message.content: Adversaries may exploit Virtual Network Computing (VNC) to remotely control machines using valid accounts. VNC is a platform-independent desktop sharing system that relays screen, mouse, and keyboard inputs over the network, differing from Remote Desktop Protocol by focusing on screen sharing rather than resource sharing. Attackers can abuse VNC to perform actions as the logged-on user, such as opening documents, downloading files, and executing arbitrary commands. This can facilitate data collection and lateral movement within a network. VNC implementations are vulnerable to brute force attacks and memory exploitation. Notable adversaries like Carberp, DanBot, FIN7, Fox Kitten, Gamaredon Group, GCMAN, Proton, TrickBot, WarzoneRAT, and ZxShell have used VNC for remote access and control. Mitigation strategies include disabling or restricting VNC usage, enforcing strong authentication mechanisms, monitoring for unusual VNC activity, and applying network segmentation to limit the spread of potential intrusions.
